summary: DeVault argues that the Commons Clause, which restricts the sale of open source software, represents an existential threat to open source by effectively converting projects to proprietary licenses. He acknowledges the real problem of open source maintainers struggling to fund their work, but contends the Commons Clause is the wrong solution because it violates the four essential freedoms. He points to alternatives like donations, hosted services, consulting, and dual licensing as better paths forward. He calls on Kevin Wang to take down the Commons Clause project entirely and urges Redis to reverse its adoption of it.
key_points: The Commons Clause removes freedom 0 (the freedom to run the program for any purpose), making software proprietary under both OSI and FSF definitions
The clause preys on a real vulnerability: open source maintainers' frustration at not being able to fund their work while companies profit from it
Viable alternatives exist including recurring donations, hosted/supported services, consulting, and dual licensing (AGPL for everyone, BSD for a price)
Contributor License Agreements enabled Redis Labs to take community contributions proprietary, demonstrating why CLAs are harmful
Businesses should fund open source projects they depend on — even small recurring donations would be transformative
money_quotes: If the Commons clause were to be adopted by all open source projects, they would cease to be open source, and therefore the Commons clause is trying to destroy open source.
It sucks to not be able to make money from your open source work. It really sucks when companies are using your work to make money for themselves.
The Commons Clause doesn't present a solution for supporting open source software. It presents a framework for turning open source software into proprietary software.
Everyone who has contributed to the now-proprietary Redis modules has had their hard work stolen and sold by RedisLabs under a proprietary license.

I do not sign CLAs and I think they're a harmful practice for this very reason. Asking a contributor to sign them is a slap in the face to the good will which led them to make a contribution in the first place.
A $250/mo recurring donation would be a drop in the bucket of most businesses, but a major boon to any open source project.
The four freedoms are important. The only solution is to bury the Commons Clause project.
As software gets abandoned, making the license more permissive is the last thing on the maintainer's minds. So as the body of Commons Clause software grows, the graveyard will only ever fill.
key_insight: The Commons Clause exploits open source maintainers' real financial struggles to lure them into making their software proprietary, and the community must instead solve the funding problem through donations, services, and business sponsorship while preserving the four essential freedoms.
